on behalf of the Marlinyu Ghoorlie Claim Group v State of Western Australia","2025","FCA","1641","19 December 2025","O\u0027Bryan J","O\u0027BRYAN J","NATIVE TITLE \u0096 overlapping applications for determination of native title in the Goldfields region of Western Australia \u0096 claims brought on behalf of the Marlinyu Ghoorlie claim group (the Karlamaya Kapurn people) and the Karratjibbin people \u0096 applications contested by indigenous respondents \u0096 trial of separate questions concerning the existence, possession and content of native title rights and interests in claim area \u0096 joint trial conducted pursuant to s 67 of the Native Title Act 1993 (Cth) \u0096 whether the Aboriginal people who occupied the claim area at effective sovereignty were the Karlamaya Kapurn people or a Western Desert society \u0096 finding that the Karlamaya Kapurn people occupied the claim area at sovereignty \u0096 the apical ancestors of the Marlinyu Ghoorlie claim group were Karlamaya Kapurn people who held rights and interests in the claim area at sovereignty \u0096 adaptations to the traditional laws and customs of the Karlamaya Kapurn people necessitated by European settlement of the claim area \u0096 the Karlamaya Kapurn people presently possess native title rights and interests in the claim area under their traditional laws and customs \u0096 the Karlamaya Kapurn people, by their traditional laws and customs, have a connection with the claim area \u0096 separate questions answered in favour of the Marlinyu Ghoorlie claim group in relation to the whole of the land and waters of the claim area \u0096 Karratjibbin claim group failed to prove that any of their claimed apical ancestors were Karlamaya Kapurn people who held rights and interests in the claim area at sovereignty\n\nEVIDENCE \u0096 significance of evidence from forebears of native title claimants who were deceased at the time of trial \u0096 where evidence from deceased forebears demonstrates ongoing vitality of the Aboriginal society since sovereignty\n\nEVIDENCE \u0096 expert anthropological evidence \u0096 principles governing admissibility and probative value of expert anthropological evidence and basis material \u0096 where expert anthropological witness relied upon written opinions of other anthropologists who were not called as witnesses and whose reports were not tendered in evidence \u0096 considerations affecting the probative value of such written opinions\n\nEVIDENCE \u0096 expert anthropological evidence \u0096 where evidence demonstrated a lack of professionalism and independence \u0096 where expert witness\u0092s contribution to the preparation of an expert report was limited","20-23 February 2023; 2-19 October 2023; 5, 7, 11-15 December 2023; Amendment Act 2009 (Qld) s 14(5)\nActs Interpretation Act 1954 (Qld) s 14A\nUniform Civil Procedure Rules 1999 (Qld) r 679, r 702, r 705, r 723, r 724","STATUTES – INTERPRETATION – where the respondent issued two notices of intention to resume land owned by the appellant – where those notices were discontinued and the land was not resumed – where the appellant contended they were entitled to various heads of loss under s 16 of the Acquisition of Land Act 1967 (Qld) (“ALA”) allegedly suffered as a consequence of the respondent’s issuance of the notices of intention to resume – where a preliminary question was submitted to the Land Court of Queensland for determination – where that preliminary question concerned whether compensation claimed under s 16(1A) of the ALA was relevantly limited to costs and expenses of a legal, valuation or other professional nature which was reasonably incurred, reasonable in amount, and was incurred in connection with the consideration or preparation of a claim for compensation – where the Court, at first instance, answered that question affirmatively – where s 16(1C) of the ALA provided that the constructing authority may have costs and expenses taxed by an officer of the Supreme Court and that no person shall be entitled to compensation in excess of the value of his or her estate or interest in the land – where the Court relevantly concluded that compensation under s 16(1A) was limited by s 16(1C) – whether the Court erred in the conclusion that the preliminary question should be answered in the AWA\nInterested Parties\nHearing:\n4–7 November 2024\n12–15 November 2024","Counsel:\nR J B Fowler KC, A J Sinclair and B M Cunningham for Whakatōhea Kotahitanga Waka (Edwards)\nM J Sharp for Ngāti NgĀTi Ira O Waioweka","Supreme Court of New Zealand","nz-sc","[2025] NZSC 0104","[2025] NZSC 104","WhakatŌHea Kotahitanga Waka (Edwards) v NgĀTi Ira O Waioweka [2025] NZSC 104","WHAKATŌHEA KOTAHITANGA WAKA (EDWARDS) \u0026 Ors v NGĀTI IRA O WAIOWEKA \u0026 Ors","WhakatŌHea Kotahitanga Waka (Edwards) v NgĀTi Ira O Waioweka  [2025] NZSC 104 (15 August 2025) (Glazebrook, Ellen France, Williams, Kós and French JJ)","2a077ce46c9f62409eecf89253bf2990","[2025] HCA 26 : MJZP v Director-General of Security","MJZP v Director-General of Security","26","6 August 2025","Gageler CJ; Gordon, Edelman, Steward, Gleeson, Jagot and Beech-Jones JJ","GAGELER CJ, GORDON, EDELMAN, STEWARD, GLEESON, JAGOT AND BEECH\u2011JONES JJ","MJZP PLAINTIFF\nDIRECTOR-GENERAL OF SECURITY \u0026 ANOR DEFENDANTS","Constitutional law (Cth) – Judicial power of Commonwealth – Where s 44(1) of Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 (Cth) (\"Act\") provided party to certain proceedings before Administrative Appeals Tribunal (\"Tribunal\") may appeal to Federal Court of Australia on question of law from decision of Tribunal – Where s 46(2) of Act provided that if certificate in force certifying disclosure of matter contained in document would be contrary to public interest Federal Court must do all things necessary to ensure matter not disclosed to any person other than member of court as constituted for purposes of proceeding – Where in accordance with s 39B of Act such certificate included certificate issued by \"ASIO Minister\" certifying disclosure of matter would be contrary to public interest because it would prejudice security or defence or international relations of Australia – Where plaintiff sought declaration that s 46(2) of Act invalid on ground it infringes Ch III of the Constitution – Where earlier decision of High Court in SDCV v Director-General of Security (2022) 277 CLR 241 held s 46(2) of Act valid – Whether s 46(2) invalid because it required Federal Court to depart from \"general rule\" of procedural fairness more than reasonably necessary to protect compelling and legitimate public interest – Whether SDCV authority to contrary – Whether leave to re-open and overrule SDCV should be granted.\n\nWords and phrases – \"adverse evidence\", \"ASIO\", \"certificate\", \"certified matter\", \"defence\", \"disclosure\", \"essential characteristic of a court\", \"forensic advantage\", \"heavy persuasive burden\", \"international relations\", \"practical injustice\", \"procedural fairness\", \"proportionality\", \"public interest immunity\", \"ratio decidendi\", \"reasonably necessary to protect a compelling and legitimate public interest\", \"security assessment\", \"special advocate\".","C L Lenehan SC and T M Wood with S N Rajanayagam for the plaintiff (instructed by Corrs Chambers Westgarth)\nS P Donaghue KC, Solicitor-General of the Commonwealth, with M A Hosking and P F Bristow for the second defendant (instructed by Australian Government Solicitor)\nG J D del Villar KC, Solicitor-General of the State of Queensland, with F J Nagorcka and K J E Blore on behalf of the Attorney-General of the State of Queensland, intervening (instructed by Crown Law (Qld))\nC S Bydder SC, Solicitor-General for the State of Western Australia, with J E Shaw SC and G M Mullins on behalf of the Attorney-General for the State of Western Australia, intervening (instructed by State Solicitor\u0027s Office (WA))\nJ G Renwick SC with M F Caristo on behalf of the Attorney-General for the State of New South Wales, intervening (instructed by Crown Solicitor for NSW)\nJ L Rudolf with E A Warner on behalf of the Attorney-General for the State of Tasmania, intervening (instructed by Solicitor-General (Tas))\nSubmitting appearance for the first defendant","Constitution, Ch III.\nAdministrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 (Cth), ss 39A, 39B, 44, 46.","The questions stated for the opinion of the Full Court in the special case filed on 20 June 2024 be answered as follows:\nQuestion (1): Is s 46(2) of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 (Cth) invalid on the basis that it infringes Ch III of the Constitution?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (2): Who should pay the costs of the Special Case and of the proceeding?\nAnswer: The plaintiff.","S142/2023","file://MJZP v Director-General of Security (S142-2023) [2025] HCA 26.docx","https://jade.io/article/src/1145922/0","Procedural Fairness","Public Interest Immunity","Constitutional Law Panel","Administrative Law Panel","[2025] HCA 0026","[2025] HCA 26","MJZP v Director-General of Security [2025] HCA 26","MJZP v Director-General of Security  [2025] HCA 26 (06 August 2025) (Gageler CJ; Gordon, Edelman, Steward, Gleeson, Jagot and Beech-Jones JJ)","423","378","423 ALR 378","1108","99 ALJR 1108","282d24e9a452f15b2a372a85a35f9b8f","Westpac Banking Corporation v Eres","NTSC","46","24 July 2025","Smyth A/AsJ","9 April 2025","2024-03087-SC","MORTGAGES – Default under a mortgage of real property – Application for order for possession – Application for summary determination for possession – Various grounds raised in opposition – Application granted.","WESTPAC BANKING CORPORATION (ACN 007 457 141) v ERES, Linda Bernadette","Counsel:\nPlaintiff: R Sanders\nDefendant: Self Represented\nSolicitors:\nPlaintiff: HWL Ebsworth Lawyers","https://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/nt/NTSC/2025/46.rtf","file:/external/2025NTSC46.rtf","NT","Northern Territory","AU_NT","NT Supreme Court","Supreme Court of Northern Territory","au-nt-sc","[2025] NTSC 0046","[2025] NTSC 46","Westpac Banking Corporation v Eres [2025] NTSC 46","Westpac Banking Corporation v Eres  [2025] NTSC 46 (24 July 2025) (Smyth A/AsJ)","ebe010d74ea68dd490bb954ef401c424","Pabai v Commonwealth of Australia (No 2)","796","15 July 2025","Wigney J","WIGNEY J","NEGLIGENCE – representative proceeding on behalf of Torres Strait Islanders against the Commonwealth of Australia – whether the Commonwealth has a duty to take reasonable steps to protect Torres Strait Islanders from the impacts of climate change – whether impacts of marine inundation and erosion caused property damage, loss of fulfilment of Ailan Kastom, and injury, disease or death – whether the loss of fulfilment of Ailan Kastom, the body of customs, traditions and beliefs distinctive to Torres Strait Islanders which creates a unique spiritual connection with the Torres Strait Islands and surrounding waters, is a compensable loss under the law of negligence – imposition of novel duties of care considered – whether posited duties of care would require the Court to pass judgment on issues of high or core government policy and political judgment – factual findings of the severe impacts of human-induced climate change on the Torres Strait Islands – ecosystems damaged and destroyed – devastating impacts on the traditional way of life and the ability to practise Ailan Kastom\n\nEMISSIONS REDUCTION TARGETS – whether Commonwealth owed the applicants and Torres Strait Islanders a duty of care to take reasonable steps to set greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets having regard to the “Best Available Science” which prevent or minimise impacts of climate change in the region – where Commonwealth set greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets and provided those targets to the UN Convention on Climate Change as Nationally Determined Contributions pursuant to Art 4.2 of the Paris Agreement in 2015, 2020, 2021 and 2022 – whether those emissions reduction targets were set having regard to the best available science – where best available science indicates global average temperature increase must be held to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels to limit the worst effects of climate change – whether setting emissions reduction targets are a matter of high or core government policy – whether relationship between the applicants and the Commonwealth more than a relationship between the governing and the governed – whether posited standard of care appropriate – whether any breach of the alleged duty of care in respect of setting emissions reduction targets caused applicants to suffer compensable damage – whether Commonwealth materially contributed to loss or damage suffered by the applicants as a result of climate change – duty of care not established – factual findings that the Commonwealth failed to give genuine consideration of the best available science – Australian emissions reduction targets inconsistent with international obligations and objectives\n\nCLIMATE CHANGE ADAPTATION – alleged duty of care to take reasonable care to protect against marine inundation and erosion – standard of care said to require the provision of predictable, adequate funding for the construction of seawalls and the leading and coordinating of a plan for funding – whether delays in funding and consequent delays in construction of seawalls cause or contributed to loss and damaged suffered – evidence incapable of proving inundation events occurring in the relevant periods caused or contributed to loss – funding arrangements concerned allocation of responsibilities between three tiers of government – all requested funding provided – delays in finalisation of funding neither solely attributable to the Commonwealth nor unreasonable – salient features do not support the alleged duty of care – duty of care not appropriate or practical to impose where the Court would need to pass judgment on the reasonableness of government policy, intergovernmental relations and budgetary priorities\n\nDECLARATIONS, INJUNCTION – declarations sought regarding the existence and breach of duty / duties of care – declaration has no utility where expressed in abstract terms divorced from the nature of any duty – inappropriate where no finding that breach has caused loss or damage – impermissible interlocutory declaration where claims of group members not finally determined – injunctive relief sought to compel the Commonwealth to implement measures to protect the environment, cultural and customary rights and reduce emissions – subjective and imprecise terms","5-6, 8-9, 12-13, 15-16 June 2023;\n8-27 November 2023;\n20 December 2023; and\n29 April-3 May 2024.","Counsel for the Applicants: Ms F McLeod AO Commonwealth of Australia","Ravbar v Commonwealth of Australia","25","18 June 2025","MICHAEL RAVBAR \u0026 ANOR PLAINTIFFS\nCOMMONWEALTH OF AUSTRALIA \u0026 ORS DEFENDANTS","Constitutional law (Cth) – Legislative power – Implied freedom of political communication – Judicial power of Commonwealth – Acquisition of property on just terms – Where Construction and General Division (\"C\u0026G Division\") of Construction, Forestry and Maritime Employees Union (\"CFMEU\") is organisation of employees registered under Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth) (\"FWRO Act\") – Where Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Amendment (Administration) Act 2024 (Cth) inserted Pt 2A of Ch 11 into FWRO Act and s 177A into Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) – Where C\u0026G Division of CFMEU and each of its branches placed under administration in accordance with Pt 2A of Ch 11 of FWRO Act – Where Attorney-General of Commonwealth determined Fair Work (Registered Organisations) (CFMEU Construction and General Division Administration) Determination 2024 (\"Scheme\") – Where Administrator of Scheme appointed – Where during ongoing administration of C\u0026G Division of CFMEU and its branches under Pt 2A of Ch 11 of FWRO Act, Administrator has and will continue to have powers of control, management and disposition of property of CFMEU previously used solely or predominantly for purposes of C\u0026G Division or any of its branches – Where plaintiffs had been office holders in C\u0026G Division of CFMEU until removed from offices as a result of Scheme – Whether impugned legislative provisions unsupported by head of Commonwealth legislative power – Whether impugned legislative provisions and/or Scheme infringed implied freedom of political communication – Whether impugned legislative provisions infringed Ch III of Constitution – Whether impugned legislative provisions effected \"acquisition of property\" within meaning of s 51(xxxi) of Constitution otherwise than on just terms.\n\nWords and phrases – \"acquisition of property\", \"administration\", \"best interests\", \"bill of pains and penalties\", \"collective will\", \"compensation\", \"constitutional corporations\", \"constitutionally prescribed system of representative government\", \"control\", \"detriments\", \"dysfunction\", \"effective burden\", \"efficient and democratic conduct\", \"extrinsic material\", \"foreseeable effects\", \"forfeiture\", \"head of power\", \"illegitimate purpose\", \"implied freedom of political communication\", \"intention\", \"invalidity\", \"judicial power\", \"legislative intent\", \"legislative purpose\", \"level of generality\", \"motive\", \"party-political donations\", \"prima facie punitive\", \"private correspondence\", \"property\", \"protective purpose\", \"public interest\", \"punishment\", \"reasonably appropriate and adapted\", \"reasonably capable of being seen as necessary\", \"shipwrecks clause\", \"single question of characterisation\", \"structured proportionality\", \"trade unions\".","B W Walker SC and C L Lenehan SC with C J Tran and N A Wootton for the plaintiffs (instructed by Hall Payne Lawyers)\nS P Donaghue KC, Solicitor-General of the Commonwealth, and N M Wood SC with C G Winnett, T M Wood and M R Salinger for the first and second defendants (instructed by Australian Government Solicitor)\nSubmitting appearance for the third defendant\nM J Wait SC, Solicitor-General for the State of South Australia, with B L Garnaut for the Attorney-General for the State of South Australia, intervening (instructed by Crown Solicitor (SA))\nS K Kay SC, Solicitor-General for the State of Tasmania, with J L Rudolf for the Attorney-General for the State of Tasmania, intervening (instructed by Solicitor-General for the State of Tasmania)\nG J D del Villar KC, Solicitor-General of the State of Queensland, with F J Nagorcka and K J E Blore for the Attorney-General of the State of Queensland, intervening (instructed by Crown Law (Qld))\nMichael Hiscox intervening, limited to written submissions","Constitution, ss 7, 24, 51(xx), 51(xxxi), Ch III.","The questions stated for the opinion of the Full Court in the special case filed on 18 October 2024 be answered as follows:\nQuestion (1): Are Pt 2A of Ch 11 of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth), s 177A of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) and/or the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Amendment (Administration) Act 2024 (Cth) invalid because they are not laws with respect to any head of power in ss 51 or 122 of the Constitution?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (2): Are Pt 2A of Ch 11 of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth), s 177A of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) and/or the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Amendment (Administration) Act 2024 (Cth) invalid because they impermissibly burden the implied freedom of political communication?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (3): Is the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) (CFMEU Construction and General Division Administration) Determination 2024 (Cth) invalid because it is not authorised by Pt 2A of Ch 11 of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth) by reason of the implied freedom of political communication?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (4): Are Pt 2A of Ch 11 of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth), s 177A of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) and/or the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Amendment (Administration) Act 2024 (Cth) invalid because they infringe Ch III of the Constitution?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (5): Are Pt 2A of Ch 11 of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth), s 177A of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) and/or the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Amendment (Administration) Act 2024 (Cth) invalid because they are laws authorising the acquisition of property otherwise than on just terms within the meaning of s 51(xxxi) of the Constitution?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (6): Is s 323M of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth) invalid because it authorises the acquisition of property otherwise than on just terms within the meaning of s 51(xxxi) of the Constitution?\nAnswer: No.\nQuestion (7): Who should bear the costs of the special case (having regard, if appropriate, to s 329 of the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth))?\nAnswer: There should be no order as to costs.","S113/2024","JOB_EXCEPTION_ARTICLE_LINK_PARSER_ACTION\u003Dtrue","file://Ravbar v Commonwealth of Australia (S113-2024) [2025] HCA Commonwealth","234","341 IR 234","bd34461d22ffdf04da85b24a20cd6a35","Replacement FCFCOA Division 1 - First Instance Judgment [SEC\u003DOFFICIAL]","Re: Blake","FedCFamC1F","364","2 June 2025","Hartnett J","HARTNETT J","FAMILY LAW – CHILDREN – Gender dysphoria – Where the respondent father did not engage with the proceedings at any stage – Where the diagnosis of gender dysphoria is supported by the Independent Children’s Lawyer – Where the child seeking to undergo treatment (“the child”) is nearly 18 years of age – Where the child seeks a change of name – Where the Court accepts the diagnosis of gender dysphoria – Declaration that the child is Gillick competent to consent to stage 2 treatment for gender dysphoria – Orders made for a change of name.","28 April 2025","By Court order the names of legal practitioners are suppressed","Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) s 140.\nFamily Law Act 1975 (Cth)\nFederal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) r 1.11","SUPPRESSED\nFEDERAL CIRCUIT AND FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA (DIVISION 1)\nBETWEEN:\nTHE MOTHER\nApplicant\nAND:\nTHE FATHER\nRespondent\nINDEPENDENT CHILDREN\u0027S LAWYER\nORDER MADE BY:\nHARTNETT J\nDATE OF ORDER:\n28 APRIL 2025 AMENDED 2 JUNE 2025\nTHE COURT ORDERS THAT:\n1. For all purposes the child JESSICA born 2007 shall be known henceforth as BLAKE.\n2. Within 28 days, the Applicant mother (“the mother”) is authorised to apply to the relevant Registrar of Births, Deaths and Marriages to change the name of the child JESSICA born 2007 to BLAKE.\n3. Pursuant to the relevant state legislation, the Registrar of Births, Death and Marriages, upon the application of the mother referred to in Order 2 hereof, is to do all acts and things to register the change of name of JESSICA born 2007 to BLAKE.\n4. The Court declares that the child Blake is Gillick competent to consent to stage 2 treatment for Gender Dysphoria in Adolescents and Adults as classified in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders 2015 (Fifth Edition) DSM-5.\n5. The proposed stage 2 treatment of the child, being the administration of testosterone in such dose, in such manner and with such frequency as defined by his treating medical practitioners, is authorised by Order of this Court.\n6. Otherwise, all extant applications are dismissed and the matter removed from the list.\n7. The name of the child, their family members and their occupations, their medical practitioners, their school, the court’s file number, the State or Territory of Australia in which these proceedings were initiated, the name of any lawyers in these proceedings and any other fact or matter that may identify the child shall not be published in any way, and only anonymised reasons for judgment and orders (with cover sheets excluding the registry, file number and lawyer’s names and details, as well as the parties’ real names) shall be released by the Court to non-parties without further contrary order of a judge, it being noted that each party shall be provided with copies of orders and any reasons for judgment with relevant details including the file number and lawyers’ names.\nAND THE COURT NOTES THAT:\nA. These orders have been amended pursuant to rule 10.13 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).\nNote: The form of the order is subject to the entry in the Court’s records.\nNote: This copy of the Court’s Reasons for judgment may be subject to review to remedy minor typographical or grammatical errors (r 10.14(b) Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth)), or to record a variation to the order pursuant to r 10.13 Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).\nPart XIVB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) makes it an offence, except in very limited circumstances, to publish an account of proceedings that identify persons, associated persons, or witnesses involved in family law v State of South Australia (Far West Coast Sea Claim) (No 4)","388","24 April 2025","NATIVE TITLE – application for a determination of native title in respect of an area in the sea in the vicinity of the far west coast of South Australia – claim area abutting and in proximity to existing determinations of native title – where applicant applied to amend the Claim Group description so as to replicate the description of native title holders in adjacent land – where determination application opposed by the State, the Commonwealth and Aboriginal respondents – whether permissible to examine intramural allocation of native title rights and interests in an already determined area – legal consequences of the adjacent determination being a judgment in rem – whether native title rights and interests are possessed in the claim area under traditional laws and customs providing for attainment of core rights by descendancy under a patrilineal estate-based tenure system that no longer exists – where traditional laws and customs have evolved to permit a greater level of cognate descent – whether native title rights and interests are held at broader levels of language groups – whether native title rights and interests exist in the claim area other than core rights acquired through cognate lines of descent – consideration of consequences of non-core rights in circumstances where the system of interdependence and interconnectedness of the former estate groups no longer exists – whether applicant discharged onus of proof in establishing connection with the claim area distinct from connection with areas already subject to native title determinations","15 – 18 March, 21 – 25 March, 28 – 31 March, 4 – 8 April, 11 – 14 April, 19 – 20 April, 13 – 15 July, 25 August, 14 December Proceeding (No 8) [2024] FCA 11\nWestern Australia v Ward (2002) 213 CLR 1\nWorimi Local Aboriginal Land Council v Minister for Lands (NSW) (No 2) (2008) 181 FCR 300\nWyman on behalf of the Bidjara People v State of Queensland (No 2) [2013] FCA 1229\nWyman on behalf of the Bidjara People v State of Queensland [2016] FCA 777\nWyman v Queensland (2015) 235 FCR 464","Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) s 191\nNative Title Act 1993 (Cth) ss 61(1), 87, 223, 225\nFederal Court Rules 2011 (Cth) r 30.01","1. The separate questions that, on 6 December 2017, the Court ordered to be determined separately from any other questions in the proceeding, be answered as follows:\n(a) But for any question of extinguishment of native title, native title exists in the land and waters of the claim area.\n(b) The persons holding the common or group rights comprising the native title are the biological or adoptive descendants of one or more of the following people:\n(i) Polly aka Polly Brown aka Polly McAvoy;\n(ii) John \u0027Jack\u0027 Bradley;\n(iii) Hanny of Emerald;\n(iv) Nannie, mother of Nelly Roberts; and\n(v) Annie/Nanny Duggan and Ned Duggan,\nwho identify as Western Kangoulu People and who are recognised as such by the Western Kangoulu People.\n(c) The nature and extent of the native title rights and interests are the following non-exclusive rights and interests in the claim area:\n(i) the right to access, be present on, move about on, and travel over the claim area;\n(ii) the right to camp on the claim area, and for that purpose, erect temporary shelters on the claim area;\n(iii) the right to take natural resources from the land and waters of the claim area for personal, domestic and non-commercial communal purposes;\n(iv) the right to take the Water of the claim area for personal, domestic and non-commercial communal purposes;\n(v) the right to maintain places of importance and areas of significance to the Western Kangoulu People under their adapted laws and customs, and protect those places and areas, from physical harm;\n(vi) the right to teach Western Kangoulu People members the physical and spiritual attributes of the claim area;\n(vii) the right to bury Western Kangoulu People members within the claim area;\n(viii) the right to assemble and conduct ceremonies and other cultural activities on the claim area; and\n(ix) the right to light fires on the claim area for cultural, spiritual or domestic purposes including cooking, but not for the purpose of hunting or clearing vegetation.\n2. In these orders, the following words have the following meanings:\n(a) Animal means any member of the animal kingdom (other than human) whether alive or dead;\n(b) claim area means the area of land and waters described in Schedules B and C of the amended native title determination application filed by the Applicant on 15 August 2017;\n(c) natural resources means:\n(i) any Animals and Plants found on or in the lands and waters of the claim area; and\n(ii) any clays, soil, sand, gravel or rock found on or below the surface of the claim area,\nthat have traditionally been taken by the Western Kangoulu People and their ancestors, but does not include:\nA. Animals that are the private personal property of any person;\nB. crops that are the private personal property of another; and\nC. minerals as defined in the Mineral Resources Act 1989 (Qld);\nD. petroleum as defined in the Petroleum Act 1923 (Qld) and the Petroleum and Gas (Production and Safety) Act 2004 (Qld);\n(d) Plant means any member of the plant or fungus kingdom, whether alive or dead and standing or fallen;\n(e) Water means:\n(i) water that flows, whether permanently or intermittently, within a river, creek or stream;\n(ii) any natural collection of water, whether permanent or intermittent; or\n(iii) water from an underground water source; and\n(f) Western Kangoulu People has the meaning given in order 1(b).\n3. The proceedings be referred to a Judicial Registrar of the Court for further mediation on the residual issues for determination.\nNote: Entry of orders is dealt with in Rule 39.32 of the Federal Court people held native title over area of land abutting claim area – Whether primary judge correctly construed and applied definition of \"native title\" in s 223(1) – Whether \"connection\" with land or waters for purposes of s 223(1)(b) must be established by physical acts of acknowledgment or observance – Relevance of native title determination in respect of land abutting claim area.\n\nWords and phrases – \"acknowledgment or observance\", \"by those laws and customs\", \"connection\", \"continuity\", \"cultural connection\", \"effective sovereignty\", \"land or waters\", \"native title\", Constitutional Law (Q.)—Reception of common law in settled colony—Effect on title of indigenous people—Annexation of territory by colony—Terra nullius—Whether doctrine applicable in Australia—Power of Parliament of Qeensland to extinguish native title. Real Property—Tenures and estates—Application on settlement of New South Wales—Effect on native title—Land over which native title exists—Whether Crown land—Land Act 1962 (Q.), s. 5—\"Crown land.\"","28-31 May 1991","In re Southern Rhodesia, (1919) A.C. 211; Amodu Tijani v. SecretQlY, Southern Nigeria, [1921] 2 A.C. 399; Adeyinka Oyekan v. Musendiku Adele, (1957] 1 W.L.R. 876; (1957] 2 All E.R. 785; Calder v. Attomey- General (British Columbia), (19731 S.C.R. 313; (1973) 34 D.L.R. (3d) 145; and Advisory Opinion on Western Sahara, (1975] I.C.J.R. 12, applied.\nAttorney-General (N.S.w.) v. Brown (1847), 1 Legge 312, at pp. 316- 318; Cooper v. Stuart (1889), 14 App. Cas. 286, at p. 291; Williams v. Attomey-General (N.S.w.) (1913), 16 c.L.R. 404, at p. 439; Vajesingii Joravarsingii v. Secretary of State for India (1924), L.R. 51 Ind. App. 357, at p. 361; Randwick Corporation v. Rutledge (1959), 102 C.L.R. 54, at p. 71; Milirrpum v. Nabalco Pty. Ltd. (\"the Gave Land Rights Case\") (1971), 17 F.L.R. 141; and New South Wales v. The Commonwealth (1981), 148 c.L.R. 1, considered.","Plaintiffs: Mabo and Ors\nDefendant: State of Queensland","A. R. Castan Q.c. (with him B. A. Keon-Cohen), for the second and third plaintiffs.\nG. O\u0027Shea, Crown Solicitor for the State of Queensland.","Mabo [No 2] [1992] HCA 23","Mabo [No 2]  [1992] HCA 23 (03 June 1992) (Mason C.J., Brennan, Deane, Dawson, Toohey, Gaudron and McHugh JJ.)","CLR","Commonwealth Law Reports","au-cr-clr","175","Mabo v Queensland (No.2)","66","408","66 ALJR 408","107","107 ALR 1","EOC","Equal Opportunity Cases","au-cr-eoc","¶92–443","(1992) EOC ¶92–443","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.JademarkCitable/2322490301","175 CLR 1","FLR","Federal Law Reports","au-cr-flr","42","42 FLR 32","Mabo [No 2]","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.csobjects.citables.CitableCitations$CitableCitation/3089345757","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.csobjects.citables.CitationInfo/810631507","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.overlay.Citation/2412779782","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.citable.CitationCitable/951721698","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.Jrl/728826604","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.ArticleVersion","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.Jrl$Instantiated/3037562470","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[123]/BLOCKQUOTE/BLOCKQUOTE/P[2]/TEXT()[3]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[123]/BLOCKQUOTE/BLOCKQUOTE/P[2]/I[1]","java.sql.Timestamp/3040052672","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.csobjects.citables.CitationPreviewData/2153957621","Para 123","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"123\"\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_sr_68\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003EMoreover, the reference to the Convention in the objects of Part VII of the \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521237\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875777\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/216646\"\u003EAct\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875777_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E reflects the position at common law. This was explained by Brereton J in \u003CI\u003ERe Thomas\u003C/I\u003E at [37] as follows:\u003C/P\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"jade-noVerticalMargins jade-tabIndent\"\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"jade-noVerticalMargins jade-tabIndent\"\u003E\u003CP class\u003D\"jade-outdent\"\u003E•\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-ts-12\"\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u003C/SPAN\u003EFirst, Australia’s ratification of CROC creates a legitimate expectation that decisions will be made having regard to the principles espoused in CROC: \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521242\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875778\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67887\"\u003EMinister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs v Teoh\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E (1995) 183 CLR 273; 128 ALR 353; 39 ALD 206; [1995] HCA 20\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875778_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E; \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521248\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875779\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-citable\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/citation/23115721\"\u003ERe B \u0026amp; B: Family Law Reform Act 1995\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E (1997) 21 Fam LR 676; 140 FLR 11;(1997) FLC 92-755\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875779_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E;\u003C/P\u003E\u003CP class\u003D\"jade-outdent\"\u003E•\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-ts-12\"\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u003C/SPAN\u003ESecond, the existence of a treaty obligation alone (that is, without legislation implementing it locally) allows a court to take such a treaty into account in the development of the common law: \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521253\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875780\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2)\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E (1992) 175 CLR 1 at \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521250\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683/section/140174\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E42\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E; 107 ALR 1 at \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521252\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683/section/2220\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E28–9\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875780_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E; \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CI\u003EB \u0026amp;B\u003C/I\u003E \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875781\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E(1997) 21 Fam LR 676 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521256\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-citable\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/citation/14864216/section/6425\"\u003E738\u003C/A\u003E; \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521257\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-citable\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/citation/14864216\"\u003E(1997) FLC 92-755\u003C/A\u003E at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521258\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-citable\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/citation/14864216/section/140353\"\u003E84\u003C/A\u003E, \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521259\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-citable\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/citation/14864216/section/140613\"\u003E223\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875781_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E (\u003CI\u003EB \u0026amp; B\u003C/I\u003E);\u003C/P\u003E\u003CP class\u003D\"jade-outdent\"\u003E•\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-ts-12\"\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u003C/SPAN\u003EThird, where a convention has been ratified by Australia but has not been the subject of any legislative incorporation into domestic law, its terms may be resorted to in order to help resolve an ambiguity in domestic legislation \u003CI\u003E(In the Marriage of Murray and Tam; Director, Family Services (ACT)\u003C/I\u003E \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875782\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E(1993) 16 Fam LR 982 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521261\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730/section/21885\"\u003E996\u003C/A\u003E; \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521262\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730\"\u003E(1993) FLC 92-416\u003C/A\u003E at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521263\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730/section/139986\"\u003E81\u003C/A\u003E, \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521264\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730/section/3034\"\u003E255–256\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875782_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E; B \u0026amp; B at Fam LR 739; FLC 84, 224), and in a case of ambiguity, a court should favour a construction of a statute which accords with Australia’s obligations under an international treaty (\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521269\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875783\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67728\"\u003EChu Kheng Lim v Minister for Immigration\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E (1992) 176 CLR 1 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521266\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67728/section/140624\"\u003E38\u003C/A\u003E; 110 ALR 97 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521268\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67728/section/4983\"\u003E122–3\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875783_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E; \u003CI\u003EB \u0026amp; B\u003C/I\u003E at Fam LR 739; FLC 84, 223). While this is not directly relevant here, it illustrates the potential for CROC to exert indirect influence on the general law;\u003C/P\u003E\u003CP class\u003D\"jade-outdent\"\u003E•\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-ts-12\"\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u003C/SPAN\u003EFourth, in the exercise of a discretion, regard may be had to an international obligation or agreement which has been ratified by Australia, though not otherwise incorporated into domestic law —unless the domestic law prohibits it: \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521276\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875784\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730\"\u003EIn the Marriage of Murray and Tam; Director, Family Services (ACT)\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E (1993) 16 Fam LR 982 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521272\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730/section/21885\"\u003E996\u003C/A\u003E; (1993) FLC 92-416 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521274\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/1091730/section/1516290\"\u003E81,255–6\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875784_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E; \u003CI\u003EB \u0026amp; B\u003C/I\u003E at Fam LR 739; FLC 84,224. This is directly relevant to discretionary decision-making in the parens patriae jurisdiction;\u003C/P\u003E\u003CP class\u003D\"jade-outdent\"\u003E•\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-ts-12\"\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u003C/SPAN\u003EFifth, in so far as the parens patriae jurisdiction overlaps the welfare jurisdiction of the Family Court of Australia, it is material that \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521277\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875785\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/216646\"\u003EFamily Law Act\u003C/A\u003E, s \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_cc_884521278\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/216646/section/27206\"\u003E43(c)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_ct_611875785_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E, provides that the court, in the exercise of its jurisdiction, must have regard to the need to protect the rights of children and to promote their welfare: \u003CI\u003EB \u0026amp; B\u003C/I\u003E at Fam LR 739; FLC 84,226 [10.7].\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1173456_e_t_sr_68\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","Para 40","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[132]/P/TEXT()[4]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[132]/P/I[2]","Para 132","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"132\"\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_sr_140167\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003EThe plurality in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794254\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310972\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68370\"\u003EYorta Yorta \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003Ewas careful to note that reference to a normative system of traditional laws and customs may be distracting if undue attention is given to the word \u0026#145;system\u0026#146;, particularly if it were to be understood as confined in its application to systems of law that have the characteristics of a European body of written laws (at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794253\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68370/section/139989\"\u003E[39]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310972_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E). Nonetheless, the plurality observed that the term recognises the fundamental premise from which the decision in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794257\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310973\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2) \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E(1992) 175 CLR 1 (\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CB\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003C/B\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E) proceeded, that the laws and customs of the indigenous peoples of Australia constituted bodies of normative rules which could give rise to, and had in fact given rise to, rights and interests in relation to land or waters (at \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794256\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683/section/140890\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E[40]\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310973_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E). Accordingly, the laws and customs in which the relevant rights or interests are founded must be laws or customs having a normative content and deriving from a body of norms or normative system that existed before sovereignty (at [38] and [42]). In \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794260\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310974\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/307219\"\u003EWyman v Queensland (No 2) \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E[2013] FCA 1229 (\u003CB\u003E\u003CI\u003EWyman (No 2)\u003C/I\u003E\u003C/B\u003E), Jagot J observed (at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794259\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/307219/section/24069\"\u003E[455]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310974_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E) that \u0026#147;normative content\u0026#148; means established behavioural norms in accordance with the recognised and acknowledged demands for conformity of a society. \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_e_t_sr_140167\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[1]/TABLE[1]/TR[11]/TD[2]/P[26]/TEXT()","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[1]/TABLE[1]/TR[11]/TD[2]/P[26]","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_cc_883794079\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310883\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2) \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E(1992) 175 CLR 1\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171535_ct_611310883_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","Mabo v QLD","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[52]/DIV/P/TEXT()[2]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[52]/DIV/P/I","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"69\"\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1171559_sr_1334\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003EIn early 2022, the Applicant commenced a relationship with Ms TB. She was also a drug user. She claims that her maternal grandfather was an aboriginal man, but she has “\u003CI\u003Elimited knowledge about her aboriginal heritage\u003C/I\u003E”.\u003CA href\u003D\"#_ftn46\" Aboriginal Australians understood, according to the tripartite test in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1170086_cc_880867295\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA name\u003D\"Catchwords\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CB\u003EMIGRATION\u003C/B\u003E – where the applicant applied for an injunction staying deportation – where the applicant is not a citizen of Australia or in Australia on a visa – where the applicant claimed to be a non-citizen – where the respondent conceded balance of convenience favours granting injunction if \u003CI\u003Eprima facie\u003C/I\u003E case demonstrated – where the applicant has produced some evidence that went to indigeneity – whether there was a serious question to be tried as to whether the applicant satisfied the tripartite test in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1170086_cc_880867261\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1170086_ct_609299214\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span submission, the respondent referred to and relied on a number of provisions of the \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_cc_877739835\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA He also referred to the implied freedom of political communication that was explained by the High Court in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_cc_877739836\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA submission, the respondent referred to and relied on a number of provisions of the \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_cc_877739835\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA He also referred to the implied freedom of political communication that was explained by the High Court in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_cc_877739836\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA Corporation\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CA href\u003D\"#_ftn1\" name\u003D\"_ftnref1\" title\u003D\"\"\u003E[1]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_ct_607260902_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67948\"\u003EKable v Director of Public Prosecutions (NSW)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA href\u003D\"#_ftn3\" name\u003D\"_ftnref3\" instruments of executive policy, and on the decision of the High Court in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_cc_877739839\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E[4]\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_ct_607260905_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E in support of the principle that the rule of law requires that legal standards apply equally to all citizens and officials.\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1162807_e_t_sr_788\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","Mabo v State of Queensland (No 2)","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[292]/P[24]/TEXT()[2]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[292]/P[24]/I[2]","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003CP\u003E[398] Ms Sinclair submitted that, as yet, the Crown has only been held to owe a fiduciary duty where there have been express undertakings or assumptions of fiduciary responsibility. In both \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_cc_878142592\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_ct_607553249\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-citable\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/citation/16241317\"\u003EGuerin \u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_ct_607553249_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003Eand \u003CI\u003EProprietors of Wakatū \u003C/I\u003Ethe fiduciary duty arose from an express assumption of responsibility, although in \u003CI\u003EProprietors of Wakatū \u003C/I\u003Ethis was through a transaction as opposed to a statute. Ms Sinclair contended that the following paragraph in the judgment of Elias CJ captured the “crux” of the reasoning on assumption of responsibility in \u003CI\u003EProprietors of Wakatū\u003C/I\u003E:\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_ct_607553250\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA href\u003D\"#_ftn286\" name\u003D\"_ftnref286\"\u003E[286]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_ct_607553250_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E \u003C/P\u003E\u003Chr\u003E\u003Cdiv class\u003D\u0027extract-via\u0027\u003E\u003Ci\u003Evia\u003C/i\u003E\u003CP class\u003D\"jade-noVerticalMargins jade-outdent\"\u003E\u003CA href\u003D\"#_ftnref286\" name\u003D\"_ftn286\"\u003E[286]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-ts-8\"\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0\u00A0Proprietors of Wakatū \u003C/I\u003E(SC), above n 49, at [366] (footnotes omitted), referring to \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_cc_878142811\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_ct_607553443\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v State of Queensland (No 2) \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E(1992) 175 CLR 1\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1164437_ct_607553443_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E. \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/div\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[1]/TABLE[1]/TR[11]/TD[2]/P[3]/TEXT()","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[1]/TABLE[1]/TR[11]/TD[2]/P[3]","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_cc_877045331\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_ct_606736744\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2)\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E (1992) 175 CLR 1\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_ct_606736744_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[4]/P/TEXT()[2]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[4]/P/A","Para 4","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"4\"\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_sr_140342\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003EIn \u003CA name\u003D\"_Hlk211842695\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_cc_877045432\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_ct_606736821\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2) \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E(1992) 175 CLR 1\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_ct_606736821_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E, the High Court confirmed that the common law of Australia recognises a form of native title that reflects the entitlement of Australia’s Indigenous inhabitants under and in accordance with their traditional laws and customs.  A purpose of the \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_cc_877045433\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_ct_606736822\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/218549\"\u003ENative Title Act\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_ct_606736822_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E was to establish special procedures for the just and proper ascertainment of native title rights and interests, and to ensure that could be done by way of conciliation or otherwise in a manner that has due regard to their unique character.\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1160294_e_t_sr_140342\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[1]/TABLE[1]/TR[11]/TD[2]/P[13]/TEXT()","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[1]/TABLE[1]/TR[11]/TD[2]/P[13]","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254695\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167553\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2) \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E(1992) 175 CLR 1\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167553_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","au.com.barnet.jade.cs.persistent.overlay.Citation$ReferenceType/1879200351","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[34]/P/TEXT()[4]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL/LI[34]/P/I[2]","Para 34","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"34\"\u003E\u003CP\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_sr_140626\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003EIt is clear that native title is extinguished by a grant of an estate in fee simple “because the rights that are given by a grant in fee simple are rights that are inconsistent with the native title holders continuing to hold any of the rights or interests which together make up native title”: \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254854\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167629\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68082\"\u003EFejo v Northern Territory of Australia \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E(1998) 195 CLR 96; [1998] HCA 58 (\u003CB\u003E\u003CI\u003EFejo\u003C/I\u003E\u003C/B\u003E) at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254852\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68082/section/1154\"\u003E126\u003C/A\u003E \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254853\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68082/section/1154\"\u003E[43]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167629_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E. As Deane and Gaudron JJ said in\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254857\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167630\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E Mabo v Queensland (No 2) \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E(1992) 175 CLR 1 at \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254856\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683/section/140351\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E110\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E,\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167630_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E in a passage that was quoted in \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254860\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167631\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68082\"\u003EFejo\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254858\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68082/section/140577\"\u003E127\u003C/A\u003E \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_cc_873254859\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/68082/section/140577\"\u003E[44]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_ct_604167631_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E:\u003C/P\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"jade-noVerticalMargins jade-tabIndent\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:94%; \"\u003EThe personal rights conferred by common law native title do not constitute an estate or interest in the land itself. They are extinguished by an unqualified grant of an inconsistent estate in the land by the Crown, such as a grant in fee or a lease conferring the right to exclusive possession.\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1155705_e_t_sr_140626\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","THE COURT","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL[1]/LI[4]/P/TEXT()[4]","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL[1]/LI[4]/P/I[3]","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"4\"\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_sr_140342\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E \u003CP\u003EAs the decision of the primary judge on the applicant’s notice of motion was interlocutory, and his Honour proceeded to give judgment on the respondent’s notice of motion for summary judgment under the rules, the applicant requires leave to appeal: \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585917\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600528\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/277064\"\u003ESupreme Court Act 1970 \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/277064\"\u003E(NSW)\u003C/A\u003E, s \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585918\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/277064/section/1724\"\u003E101(2)(e)\u003C/A\u003E and s \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585919\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/277064/section/32248\"\u003E101(2)(l)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600528_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E. Generally speaking, it is only appropriate to grant leave to appeal in matters that involve issues of principle, questions of public importance or in circumstances where it is reasonably clear that an injustice has occurred by reason of error in the judgment, going beyond what is merely arguable: \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585923\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600529\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/290296\"\u003EThe Age Company Ltd v Liu\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E (2013) 82 NSWLR 268; [2013] NSWCA 26 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585922\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/290296/section/140536\"\u003E[13]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600529_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E. The applicant submitted that this proceeding was not an ordinary possession matter because it raised important constitutional questions, including but not limited to the implications of the High Court’s decision in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585926\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600530\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo v Queensland (No 2)\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E (1992) 175 CLR 1; [1992] HCA 23\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600530_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E (“\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CI\u003EMabo (No\u00A02)\u003C/I\u003E”). Although he brought the application for leave to appeal in this Court, he submitted that the issues raised were beyond this Court’s jurisdiction and that only the High Court could decide them.\u003C/P\u003E \u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_e_t_sr_140342\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL[1]/LI[5]/P/I[1]/TEXT()","/HTML/BODY/DIV/OL[1]/LI[5]/P/I[1]","Para 5","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"5\"\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_sr_482\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E \u003CP\u003EThe applicant’s submission as to the limits of this Court’s jurisdiction was incorrect. However, it is the case this Court is bound by decisions of the High Court, including \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585927\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600531\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600531_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E, which requires rejection of the applicant’s central contention that there is a form of Indigenous title to land that is distinct from native title and that survives a grant of fee simple registered under the \u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872585928\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600532\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/276819\"\u003EReal Property Act 1900 \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/276819\"\u003E(NSW)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600532_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E. The primary judge’s conclusion that the applicant’s defence (and proposed amended defence) was futile in so far as it sought to raise such arguments was not attended by error.\u003C/P\u003E \u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_e_t_sr_482\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/body\u003E\u003C/html\u003E","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[12]/BLOCKQUOTE/BLOCKQUOTE/P/I/TEXT()","/HTML/BODY/DIV/BLOCKQUOTE[12]/BLOCKQUOTE/BLOCKQUOTE","Para 37","\u003Chtml\u003E\u003Chead\u003E\u003Cmeta http-equiv\u003D\u0027Content-Type\u0027 content\u003D\"text/html; charset\u003DUTF-8\"\u003E\u003Ctitle\u003EContent extract - BarNet Jade\u003C/title\u003E\u003C/head\u003E\u003Cbody\u003E\u003COL start\u003D\"35\"\u003E\u003CLI value\u003D\"37\"\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_sr_140276\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E \u003CP\u003EThe primary judge’s rejection of the applicant’s contentions was consistent with the decision of the High Court in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586014\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600581\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600581_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E. True it is, as the applicant submitted, that the High Court rejected the doctrine of terra nullius in that case. However, the Court also rejected a concept of Indigenous sovereignty adverse to the Crown. At first instance in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586018\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600582\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/956885\"\u003EIndigenous Land and Sea Corporation v Anderson \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E[2022] NSWSC 1650; 406 ALR 484 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586017\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/956885/section/2883\"\u003E[32]-[34]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600582_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E, Griffiths AJ referred with approval to the reasoning of Mansfield J in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586022\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600583\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/298938\"\u003EWalker v South Australia (No 2) \u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E(2013) 215 FCR 254; [2013] FCA 700 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586021\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/298938/section/17272\"\u003E[43]-[47]\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600583_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E, where his Honour stated: \u003C/P\u003E \u003C/LI\u003E\u003C/OL\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003E“[43] The contention that the Indigenous peoples of Australia constitute a sovereign nation or nations, has been expressly rejected. In \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586026\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600584\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/188346\"\u003ECoe v Commonwealth\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E [1979] HCA 68; (1979) 53 ALJR 403 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586025\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/188346/section/140102\"\u003E408\u003C/A\u003E,\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600584_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E Gibbs J, with whom Aicken J agreed, held:\u003C/P\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003EThe aboriginal people are subject to the laws of the Commonwealth and of the States or Territories in which they respectively reside. They have no legislative, executive or judicial organs by which sovereignty might be exercised. If such organs existed, they would have no powers, except such as the laws of the Commonwealth, or of a State or Territory, might confer upon them. The contention that there is in Australia an aboriginal nation exercising sovereignty, even of a limited kind, is quite impossible in law to maintain.\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003E[44] That is consistent with \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586027\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600585\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600585_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E. The rights recognised with respect to native title in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586028\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600586\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003EMabo (No 2) \u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600586_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003Eare, at common law, subject to statutory modification: see at 110–111 per Deane and Gaudron JJ:\u003C/P\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003ELike other legal rights, including rights of property, the rights conferred by common law native title and the title itself can be dealt with, expropriated or extinguished by valid Commonwealth, State or Territorial legislation operating within the State or Territory in which the land in question is situated. To put the matter differently, the rights are not entrenched in the sense that they are, by reason of their nature, beyond the reach of legislative power.\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003E[45] \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586030\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600587\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E reveals that, upon the settlement in New South Wales, and by extension later in South Australia, the English settlers brought with them the law of England, so that, at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586029\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683/section/140624\"\u003E38\u003C/A\u003E per Brennan J\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600587_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E:\u003C/P\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003E[t]he common law thus became the common law of all subjects within the Colony who were equally entitled to the law’s protection as subjects of the Crown. ... Thus the Meriam people in 1879, like Australian Aborigines in earlier times, became British subjects owing allegiance to the Imperial Sovereign entitled to such rights and privileges and subject to such liabilities as the common law and applicable statutes provided.\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003E[46] Mason CJ in \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586034\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600588\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67773\"\u003ECoe v Commonwealth\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E [1993] HCA 42; (1993) 68 ALJR 110 at \u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586033\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67773/section/140874\"\u003E116\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600588_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E confirmed that \u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586035\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600589\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600589_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E is inconsistent with the notion of sovereignty in the Aboriginal people of Australia:\u003C/P\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%; \"\u003E\u003CI\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_cc_872586036\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600590\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA class\u003D\"bn_wrapped_span jade-out-article\" href\u003D\"https://jade.io/article/67683\"\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003EMabo (No 2)\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003CA id\u003D\"bnj_a_1154474_ct_603600590_e_t\"\u003E\u003C/A\u003E\u003C/I\u003E\u003CSPAN class\u003D\"jade-incoming-citation\"\u003E is entirely at odds with the notion that sovereignty adverse to the Crown resides in the Aboriginal people of Australia. The decision is equally at odds with the notion that there resides in the Aboriginal people a limited kind of sovereignty embraced in the notion that they are “a domestic dependent nation” entitled to self-government and full rights (save the right of alienation) or that as a free and independent people they are entitled to any rights and interests other than those created or recognised by the laws of the Commonwealth, the State of New South Wales and the common law.\u003C/SPAN\u003E\u003C/P\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003C/BLOCKQUOTE\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CBLOCKQUOTE class\u003D\"\"\u003E\u003CP style\u003D\"font-size:88%;
